perturbation.original_text: Guarantors shall unconditionally guarantee full performance and discharge by Master Franchisee of all the obligations of Master Franchisee under the Master Franchise Agreement at the times and in the manner therein provided.
perturbation.changed_text: Guarantors shall endeavor to guarantee partial performance of the Master Franchisee's obligations under the Master Franchise Agreement as they deem reasonable.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'unconditionally guarantee full performance' to 'endeavor to guarantee partial performance as they deem reasonable' significantly weakens the guarantee, making it subjective and potentially unenforceable. This contradicts the typical expectations and legal interpretations of a guarantee agreement.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Statute of Frauds - Requirement of a Guarantee to be in Writing and Unconditional
perturbation.law_explanation: New York's Statute of Frauds, similar to those in other states, requires a guarantee to be in writing and clearly express the guarantor's intent to be bound. By making the guarantee 'partial' and based on what the guarantors 'deem reasonable,' the modified text creates ambiguity and weakens the enforceability of the guarantee, potentially violating the statute of frauds. A true guarantee is expected to be unconditional.

Skip to main content Legislation Search OpenLegislation Statutes Search Term Search icon Search The Laws of New York Consolidated Laws of New York CHAPTER 24-A General Obligations ARTICLE 5 Creation, Definition and Enforcement of Contractual Obligations TITLE 7 Requirements of Writing, Execution or Acknowledgment For Effectiveness or Enforceability up TITLE 7 Requirements of Writing, Execution or Acknowledgment For Effectiveness or Enforceability next SECTION 5-702 Requirements for use of plain language in consumer transactions This entry was published on 2014-09-22 The selection dates indicate all change milestones for the entire volume, not just the location being viewed. Specifying a milestone date will retrieve the most recent version of the location before that date. See most recent version before or on: 2014-09-22 2015-10-09 2017-10-27 2018-04-20 2018-07-06 2018-07-13 2018-08-31 2019-01-11 2019-01-18 2019-06-28 2019-07-05 2019-07-19 2019-08-16 2019-10-18 2019-11-22 2019-12-13 2020-02-21 2020-04-10 2020-12-18 2021-01-08 2021-04-02 2021-04-09 2021-06-18 2021-09-24 2021-12-31 2022-02-25 2022-03-04 2022-12-30 2023-01-06 2023-10-27 2023-11-26 2024-12-20 2025-01-03 Share Facebook Email SECTION 5-701 Agreements required to be in writing General Obligations (GOB) CHAPTER 24-A, ARTICLE 5, TITLE 7 § 5-701. Agreements required to be in writing. a. Every agreement, promise or undertaking is void, unless it or some note or memorandum thereof be in writing, and subscribed by the party to be charged therewith, or by his lawful agent, if such agreement, promise or undertaking: 1. By its terms is not to be performed within one year from the making thereof or the performance of which is not to be completed before the end of a lifetime; 2. Is a special promise to answer for the debt, default or miscarriage of another person; 3. Is made in consideration of marriage, except mutual promises to marry; 5. Is a subsequent or new promise to pay a debt discharged in bankruptcy; 6. Notwithstanding section 2-201 of the uniform commercial code, if the goods be sold at public auction, and the auctioneer at the time of the sale, enters in a sale book, a memorandum specifying the nature and price of the property sold, the terms of the sale, the name of the purchaser, and the name of the person on whose account the sale was made, such memorandum is equivalent in effect to a note of the contract or sale, subscribed by the party to be charged therewith; 9. Is a contract to assign or an assignment, with or without consideration to the promisor, of a life or health or accident insurance policy, or a promise, with or without consideration to the promisor, to name a beneficiary of any such policy. This provision shall not apply to a policy of industrial life or health or accident insurance. 10. Is a contract to pay compensation for services rendered in negotiating a loan, or in negotiating the purchase, sale, exchange, renting or leasing of any real estate or interest therein, or of a business opportunity, business, its good will, inventory, fixtures or an interest therein, including a majority of the voting stock interest in a corporation and including the creating of a partnership interest.
perturbation.justification: The modified text significantly weakens the guarantee provided by the guarantors, potentially contradicting legal expectations for guarantee agreements. The original text states 'Guarantors shall unconditionally guarantee full performance,' indicating a clear and absolute obligation. The changed text, 'Guarantors shall endeavor to guarantee partial performance as they deem reasonable,' introduces ambiguity and discretion, making the guarantee subjective and less enforceable. Scraped Law Snippet 1, specifically SECTION 5-701, addresses agreements required to be in writing, including 'a special promise to answer for the debt, default or miscarriage of another person.' While this snippet doesn't explicitly define the required strength of a guarantee, the Law Explanation states that a true guarantee is expected to be unconditional, which aligns with the original text but is undermined by the changed text's ambiguity and partial commitment. The change from 'unconditionally guarantee full performance' to 'endeavor to guarantee partial performance as they deem reasonable' alters the fundamental nature of the guarantee, creating a legal contradiction, falling under the 'Misaligned Terminology - Legal Contradiction' definition, since the change deviates from the legally recognized expectation of a guarantee.

perturbation.original_text: Franchisor shall not be obligated to proceed against Master Franchisee or exhaust any security from Master Franchisee or pursue or exhaust any remedy, including any legal or equitable relief against Master Franchisee, before proceeding to enforce the obligations of the Guarantors under this Guarantee, and the enforcement of such obligations may take place before, after, or contemporaneously with, enforcement of any debt or obligation of Master Franchisee under the Master Franchise Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: Franchisor must first exhaust all remedies against Master Franchisee before seeking recourse from the Guarantors; enforcement can only begin after Master Franchisee is proven unable to fulfill obligations.
perturbation.explanation: The original clause allows the Franchisor to pursue the Guarantors without first exhausting remedies against the Master Franchisee. The modified text reverses this, requiring the Franchisor to exhaust all remedies against the Master Franchisee first. This fundamentally alters the guarantor's responsibility and is a deviation from standard guarantee agreements where immediate recourse against the guarantor is often permitted.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Civil Code § 2845 - Surety's Right to Compel Creditor to Sue
perturbation.law_explanation: While California Civil Code § 2845 grants a surety (guarantor) the *right to compel* the creditor (franchisor) to proceed against the principal (master franchisee) first, the original contract *waives* this right. The changed text, mandating the franchisor to exhaust remedies against the master franchisee, effectively reinstates a right that was explicitly waived and changes the nature of the guarantee.  The modification could conflict with the enforceability of the waiver provision.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CIV 2845. skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> Code Search >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 3. OBLIGATIONS [1427 - 3273.69] ( Heading of Division 3 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 14. ) PART 4. OBLIGATIONS ARISING FROM PARTICULAR TRANSACTIONS [1738 - 3273.69] ( Part 4 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 13. SURETYSHIP [2787 - 2856] ( Heading of Title 13 amended by Stats. 1939, Ch. 453. ) ARTICLE 7. Position of Sureties [2832 - 2856] ( Heading of Article 7 renumbered from Article 1 (of former Chapter 2) by Stats. 1939, Ch. 453. ) 2845. A surety may require the creditor, subject to Section 996.440 of the Code of Civil Procedure, to proceed against the principal, or to pursue any other remedy in the creditor’s power which the surety cannot pursue, and which would lighten the surety’s burden; and if the creditor neglects to do so, the surety is exonerated to the extent to which the surety is thereby prejudiced. (Amended by Stats. 1982, Ch. 517, Sec. 73.)
perturbation.justification: The original contract allows the Franchisor to pursue the Guarantors immediately, waiving the need to exhaust remedies against the Master Franchisee. The modified text reverses this, compelling the Franchisor to first exhaust all remedies against the Master Franchisee. This directly contradicts the original agreement's waiver of the surety's (Guarantor's) right to compel the creditor (Franchisor) to proceed against the principal (Master Franchisee) first, as described in the Law Explanation. While California Civil Code § 2845, as shown in Scraped Law Snippet 1, does allow a surety to request the creditor to proceed against the principal first, the original agreement explicitly waived this right. The changed text effectively reinstates this waived right, creating a legal contradiction because the original agreement allowed the franchisor to pursue the guarantor without exhausting remedies against the master franchisee first.

perturbation.original_text: This Guarantee shall be interpreted and construed under the laws of the state in which Franchisor has its principal place of business at the time the action is initiated. In the event of any conflict of law, the laws of such state shall prevail, without regard to the application of such state's conflict of law rules. If, however, any provision of this Guarantee would not be enforceable under the laws of the state in which Franchisor has its principal place of business at the time the action is initiated, and if the Master Franchisee's franchised business is located outside of such state and such provision would be enforceable under the laws of the state in which the Master Franchisee's franchised business is located, then such provision shall be interpreted and construed under the laws of that state.
perturbation.changed_text: This Guarantee shall be interpreted at the Guarantor's discretion, following principles of equity and fairness, regardless of the Franchisor's principal place of business. Conflicts will be resolved through arbitration determined by the Guarantor.
perturbation.explanation: The original clause specifies a clear choice-of-law provision, prioritizing the Franchisor's principal place of business. The modified text replaces this with a vague standard of 'equity and fairness' interpreted at the Guarantor's discretion. This creates significant uncertainty about which laws govern the agreement, contradicting established contract law principles related to choice of law.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Restatement (Second) of Conflict of Laws § 187 - Law of the State Chosen by the Parties
perturbation.law_explanation: Restatement (Second) of Conflict of Laws § 187 generally upholds choice-of-law provisions in contracts, provided there is a reasonable basis for the choice and it doesn't violate a fundamental policy of a state with a materially greater interest. By replacing the specific choice-of-law clause with a vague standard subject to the Guarantor's discretion, the modified text undermines the enforceability of the choice-of-law provision and creates uncertainty, directly conflicting with the principles outlined in the Restatement.
perturbation.justification: The original text specifies a choice-of-law provision, which the Law Explanation and Perturbation Explanation state is generally upheld under Restatement (Second) of Conflict of Laws § 187. The changed text replaces this with a vague standard, giving discretion to the Guarantor. This undermines the enforceability of the choice-of-law provision and creates uncertainty, contradicting principles that support such provisions.
